proposal release genetically engineered (GE) moths area upstate York been approved U.S. Department Agriculture (USDA).
trial meant test efficacy moths reducing local diamondback moth population.
Diamondback moths considered invasive pest because they destroy vegetables like cabbage, cauliflower broccoli when they're caterpillar stage their life cycle.
moths designed before adulthood.
After male moths released into test site, their gene passed offspring through mating.
field trial team researchers from Cornell University plan release moths 10-acre field Geneva, York, owned university.
federal permit allows 30,000 moths released week over period three four months.
According U.K. company that produces moths, Oxitec, moths alternative insecticides control pests which also often eradicate other more desirable insects integral ecosystem, like bees other pollinators.
same company submitted withdrawn applications field trials insects Spain elsewhere battle olive fruit fly.
However, Washington-based Center Food Safety said that genetically engineered insects threat "native ecosystems human health" with potential create problems like harm other species that rely them food.
group York organic farmers also opposed experiment calling full environmental review.
According Northeast Organic Farming Association York (NOFA-NY), USDA's environmental assessment sufficiently addressed potential health environmental effects.
NOFA-NY's policy advisor, Liana Hoodes, told EcoWatch that York State Department Environmental Conservation (DEC) require environmental impact assessment.
NOFA-NY also pointed that Oxitec completed thorough health, safety environmental review before exporting moths.
2016, approval granted release Florida mosquitoes, also produced Oxitec, test their effectiveness eradicating Aedes aegypti mosquito, responsible transmitting viruses like Zika, dengue fever chikungunya.
This proposed trial been delayed following opposition from local residents.
However, company been successful releasing mosquitoes Brazil, Cayman Islands Panama with reports subsequent decline Aedes aegypti mosquito population.

clasped hand over mouth nose into open living room dining area.

entire entry ablaze, fire sweeping across floor.

passed kitchen, heart stuttered.
even worse there.

heart slammed against cage erratic rhythm.
Someone trapped inside.
tried tamp down panic.
building probably shifted, binding door.
just needed force open.
stepped back threw weight against
didn't move.
Something seemed holding closed from outside.
Panic flared anew.
lungs burned, body screamed oxygen.
Unable hold breath longer, grabbed fistful shirt pressed face.
When drew gulp air, throat closed reflex, coughing drove knees.
With shaking hands, dialed 911.
When dispatcher answered, could manage raspy croak more coughing.
After dropping phone, rose, stumbled nearest window flipped locks into open position.
wouldn't budge.
wooden window frame been sealed permanently shut decades painting repainting.
others were same way.
last desperate attempt escape, picked chair slammed against window.
She'd have break more than just glass.
wood that separated six-by-nine panes would have destroyed before could climb safety.
Glass shattered wood splintered.
Each effort brought more coughing.
Ivan.
He'd warned her.
couldn't have her, he'd make sure else could, either.
restraining order meant nothing.
God, please help
sirens died away.
feet away, back door swung open, flashlight beam swept through smoke.
"Over here."
gloved grasped fireman dropped knees beside her.
Strong arms slid beneath lifted.
squinted, trying face behind plastic shield.
darkness encroached further.
